titleEn: We Drank upon the Remembrance of the Beloved (al-Khamriyya)

verses.ajuz: سَكِرْنَا بِهَا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يُخْلَقَ الكَرْمُ

verses.sadr: لَهَا البَدْرُ كَأْسٌ وَهْيَ شَمْسٌ يُدِيرُهَا
verses.ajuz: هِلَالٌ وَكَمْ يَبْدُو إِذَا مُزِجَتْ نَجْمُ
وَلَوْلَا سَنَاهَا مَا تَصَوَّرَهَا الوَهْمُ

verses.sadr: وَلَمْ يُبْقِ مِنْهَا الدَّهْرُ غَيْرَ حُشَاشَةٍ
verses.ajuz: كَأَنَّ خَفَاهَا فِي صُدُورِ النُّهَى كَتْمُ

verses.sadr: فَإِنْ ذُكِرَتْ فِي الحَيِّ أَصْبَحَ أَهْلُهُ
verses.ajuz: نَشَاوَى وَلَا عَارٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا إِثْمُ
وَلَمْ يَبْقَ مِنْهَا فِي الحَقِيقَةِ إِلَّا اسْمُ

verses.sadr: وَإِنْ خَطَرَتْ يَوْمًا عَلَى خَاطِرِ امْرِئٍ
verses.ajuz: أَقَامَتْ بِهِ الأَفْرَاحُ وَارْتَحَلَ الهَمُّ
لَأَسْكَرَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهَا ذَلِكَ الخَتْمُ

translationEn: We drank upon the remembrance of the Beloved a wine with which we became intoxicated before the vine was created. The full moon is its cup, and it is a sun that a crescent circulates — and how many stars appear when it is mixed! Were it not for its fragrance, I would not have found my way to its tavern; were it not for its radiance, the imagination could not picture it. Time has left of it only a last breath, as though its hiddenness in the breasts of the wise were concealment. If it is merely mentioned in the tribe, the people awaken intoxicated — and upon them is no shame nor sin. From within the bowels of the wine-jars it ascended, and in reality nothing of it remains but a name. If it merely crosses the mind of a person, joy takes up residence in him and sorrow departs. If the drinking companions were to gaze upon the seal of its vessel, that seal alone would intoxicate them without the wine itself.
context: The most celebrated Sufi poem in Arabic literature, al-Khamriyya (The Wine Ode) by Ibn al-Farid uses wine as an extended metaphor for divine love and spiritual intoxication. The wine here is not physical — it existed before the creation of the grapevine itself. This pre-eternal wine represents divine knowledge and love that the mystic "drinks" through dhikr (remembrance of God). The poem influenced Sufi literature across all Islamic languages.

titleEn: My Heart Tells Me You Are My Destroyer

verses.ajuz: رُوحِي فِدَاكَ عَرَفْتَ أَمْ لَمْ تَعْرِفِ

verses.sadr: لَمْ أَقْضِ حَقَّ هَوَاكَ إِنْ كُنْتُ الَّذِي
verses.ajuz: لَمْ أَقْضِ فِيهِ أَسًى وَمِثْلِي مَنْ يَفِي

verses.sadr: مَا لِي سِوَى رُوحِي وَبَاذِلُ نَفْسِهِ
verses.ajuz: فِي حُبِّ مَنْ يَهْوَاهُ لَيْسَ بِمُسْرِفِ
يَا خَيْبَةَ المُسْعَى إِذَا لَمْ تُسْعِفِ
لَكَ مِنْ مَعَانِي الحُسْنِ مَا لَمْ يُوصَفِ

translationEn: My heart tells me that you are my destroyer — my soul is your ransom, whether you know it or not. I have not fulfilled the right of your love if I am one who has not perished in it with grief, and one like me is faithful. I have nothing but my soul, and one who sacrifices himself for the love of the one he loves is not extravagant. If you accept it, then you have granted me mercy — O what a loss of effort if you do not! O You endowed with beauty and characterized by it, Yours are meanings of beauty beyond description.
context: One of Ibn al-Farid's most passionate expressions of divine love. On the surface it reads as a love poem to a human beloved, but in Sufi interpretation the "You" addressed is God. The willingness to be destroyed by the Beloved — and the declaration that sacrificing one's soul for love is not extravagance — captures the fana (annihilation in God) central to Sufi mysticism. Ibn al-Farid earned the title "Sultan al-Ashiqin" (Sultan of Lovers) for poems like this.

titleEn: Increase My Bewilderment in Excessive Love of You

verses.ajuz: وَارْحَمْ حَشًا بِلَظَى هَوَاكَ تَسَعَّرَا
فَاسْمَحْ وَلَا تَجْعَلْ جَوَابِي لَنْ تَرَى

verses.sadr: يَا قَلْبُ إِنْ ضَاقَتْ عَلَيْكَ فَلَا تَضِقْ
verses.ajuz: فَوَرَاءَ ضِيقِ الحَالِ بَابٌ يُفْتَحُ
بِعُرَى رَجَاءٍ لَيْسَ يَنْقَطِعُ وَلَا

translationEn: Increase my bewilderment in excessive love of You, and have mercy on a heart set ablaze by the fire of Your love. And when I ask You to see You in reality, be gracious and do not make my answer "You shall never see" (as God told Moses). O heart, if things become constricted upon you, do not despair — for beyond every constriction is a door that opens. And patience is the key to relief, so hold fast to the handholds of a hope that never ceases.
context: This poem references the Quranic episode where Prophet Musa (Moses) asked to see God and was told "You shall not see Me" (lan tarani, Quran 7:143). Ibn al-Farid pleads that when he asks to see the Divine Beloved, the answer not be the same refusal. This blending of Quranic allusion with passionate love poetry is the hallmark of his mystical style. The poem is widely recited in Sufi gatherings (hadras) across the Muslim world.

titleAr: هَلْ نَارُ لَيْلَى بَدَتْ لَيْلًا بِذِي سَلَمِ
titleEn: Did the Fire of Layla Appear by Night at Dhi Salam?

verses.sadr: هَلْ نَارُ لَيْلَى بَدَتْ لَيْلًا بِذِي سَلَمِ
verses.ajuz: أَمْ بَارِقٌ لَاحَ فِي الزَّوْرَاءِ فَالعَلَمِ

verses.sadr: أَمِ النُّورُ نُورُ اللَّهِ لَاحَ لِنَاظِرِي
verses.ajuz: فَأَشْرَقَ حَتَّى عَمَّ كُلَّ الظُّلَمِ
عَلَى ظَمَأٍ مِنِّي لِذَاكَ وَمِنْ كَرَمِ
وَقَالَ بِلِسَانِ الحَالِ لَا بِالكَلَمِ

translationEn: Did the fire of Layla appear by night at Dhi Salam, or was it lightning that flashed over al-Zawra and the highland? Or was it the light of God that shone before my eyes, blazing until it overwhelmed all darkness? He offered her and greeted her with the purity of affection, upon my thirst for that — and out of generosity. My Beloved manifested in the mirrors of His beauty, and spoke in the tongue of spiritual states, not in words.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Dhi Salam, a place with salam trees

context: This poem begins with the traditional Bedouin motif of seeing a distant fire or lightning and asking whether it belongs to the beloved's camp — but Ibn al-Farid transforms it into a vision of divine light (nur). The "fire of Layla" becomes the fire of divine self-disclosure (tajalli). The final verse, where the Beloved speaks "in the tongue of states, not words," expresses the Sufi concept of hal — spiritual states beyond verbal expression.

titleEn: O Driver of the Camel Litters, Folding the Desert

verses.ajuz: مَنْ بِالغَضَا أَنْتَ مَحْسُودٌ عَلَى القُرْبِ
هَانَ الفِرَاقُ عَلَى ذِي لَوْعَةٍ صَبِّ
لَوْ كَانَ يُمْكِنُ أَنْ يُسَارَ عَلَى الرِّيحِ
لَكُمُ وَأَنْتُمْ فِي الفُؤَادِ نُزُولُ

verses.sadr: هَلْ مِنْ رَسُولٍ يُخْبِرُ الأَحْبَابَ عَنْ
verses.ajuz: أَحْوَالِنَا فَلَعَلَّ ذَاكَ يَعُودُ

translationEn: O driver of the camel litters, folding the desert — you who are near those at the thicket, you are envied for that closeness! Be gentle with the people of love, be gentle — for separation is not easy upon one with burning passion and ardent love. If I could, I would ride the east wind toward you — if only one could travel upon the breeze. O you who depart, yet in our hearts are dwellings for you, and you are guests within the heart! Is there any messenger to tell the beloveds of our conditions? Perhaps that would bring them back.
context: In this poem, Ibn al-Farid employs the classical Arabic motif of addressing the caravan driver (saa'iq al-az'an) — a figure who takes the beloved away. In Sufi reading, the departing caravan represents the soul's separation from the Divine Presence, and the plea for gentleness is a prayer for spiritual reunion. The wish to ride the wind is an image of the soul's yearning to transcend physical limitations to reach God.
